summary: Audit of :core-settings module (commit 5de04cd). One critical finding: protobuf-javalite 4.26.1 is affected by CVE-2024-7254 (stack overflow / memory exhaustion via crafted proto input). All other audit points pass cleanly: DataStore is app-private, serializer is memory-safe, backup is already disabled in :app manifest, the self-closing library manifest does not add an <application> element so it cannot override allowBackup, ErrorLog sanitises and truncates messages before writing, modelId keys are internal-trust and unbounded length is inherent to proto map semantics (low risk), Hilt binding is correctly singleton, no hardcoded secrets, no PII in logs, test hygiene is good.

findings.title: protobuf-javalite 4.26.1 affected by CVE-2024-7254 (stack overflow / DoS)
findings.description: CVE-2024-7254 (CVSS 7.5, HIGH) affects com.google.protobuf:protobuf-javalite for all 4.x versions prior to 4.27.2 and all 3.x versions prior to 3.25.5. Specially crafted protobuf messages with deeply nested group fields can trigger a stack overflow, causing a denial-of-service crash of the process. In this app the DataStore reads persisted .pb files from app-private storage, so an attacker would need to pre-corrupt the file (e.g. via a root exploit) to trigger the path. However, if the app ever reads proto bytes from any network or IPC source in future, the risk becomes directly exploitable. The safe fix is to upgrade to protobuf 4.28.0+ (stable) or at minimum 4.27.2.
findings.recommendation:
Upgrade protobuf version in libs.versions.toml from 4.26.1 to 4.28.3 (latest stable as of 2026). The protobuf-javalite and protoc artifacts share the same version string, so changing the single `protobuf` version entry covers both. Verify generated proto API is unchanged (hasXxx / getXxx remain stable between 4.26 and 4.28).

In libs.versions.toml:
  protobuf = "4.28.3"  # was 4.26.1

findings.title: CorruptionException not caught in observePerModelSettings; silent data-loss on corruption during save/reset
findings.description: The observePerModelSettings Flow's .catch block only handles IOException. DataStore internally wraps InvalidProtocolBufferException as CorruptionException (a RuntimeException, not IOException), so if AppSettingsSerializer throws CorruptionException, it propagates uncaught through the observer and crashes the collector. In the current wiring, DataStore itself catches CorruptionException at the DataStore layer and will re-emit its defaultValue; however that behaviour is DataStore-internal and not guaranteed to surface as IOException to the .catch block, meaning the user-visible behaviour and the error logging are both implementation-defined. Additionally, savePerModelSettings and resetPerModelSettings catch IOException but not CorruptionException, so a corrupt file discovered during updateData could surface as an unhandled RuntimeException. The resulting silent data-loss (settings silently not saved) is a tradeoff acknowledged in the tech-spec, but the missing log entry means operators cannot observe the event.

findings.title: modelId string is unbounded with no length or character validation at the API boundary
findings.description: The public interface accepts a bare String modelId with no length cap or character-set restriction. In the current codebase modelId comes from a bundled allowlist (internal trust boundary), making active exploitation impractical. However, the repository API is a module-level contract and could be called by future feature modules with externally-derived modelIds. A very long modelId (e.g. 64 KB) would be stored as a proto map key and written to the DataStore file, bloating the .pb on every save. The proto map key encoding is binary-safe so there is no proto injection risk, but a runaway key size is a latent DoS vector. ErrorLog already sanitises the modelId in the log message (500-char truncation), so there is no log-injection risk.
